entries.title: Proxy overview: how MITM works
entries.body:
# Proxy - the heart of Burp Suite

**Proxy** is the first tool you open at the start of any test, and the one through which almost all of your traffic passes. At its core it is a local proxy server (by default it listens on `127.0.0.1:8080`) that sits between the browser and the target application. The browser thinks it is talking to the site directly, the site thinks it is replying to the browser, and in reality Burp sits in the middle and shows you every request and response. This is the classic **man-in-the-middle (MITM)** scheme, only carried out with your own consent and on your own machine.

Why you need this in practice:

- To see the **real** HTTP traffic, not what DevTools shows. Burp catches absolutely everything: background XHR/fetch, preloads, Service Worker requests, telemetry, calls to third-party APIs.
- To intercept and **edit requests and responses on the fly** (see **Intercept**).
- To stack everything into **HTTP history**, from where it is convenient to send interesting requests to **Repeater** and **Intruder**.
- To automatically modify traffic with **Match and replace** rules.
- To work with HTTPS - for this Burp swaps TLS certificates on the fly (see the section on the CA certificate).

## Burp's browser versus an external browser

You have two ways to route traffic into Burp.

**1. The built-in browser (Burp's browser).** On the **Proxy > Intercept** tab click **Open browser** - a Chromium will open, already configured for the Burp proxy, with the CA certificate already trusted. There is nothing to configure, HTTPS works out of the box. This is the fastest and most trouble-free way; I recommend starting with it, especially for beginners.

```
Proxy → Intercept → [Open browser]
```

**2. An external browser (your Firefox/Chrome).** Sometimes you need exactly your profile - with logged-in accounts, extensions, bookmarks. Then you point the system/browser proxy at `127.0.0.1:8080` and **separately** install Burp's CA certificate into the browser's store (otherwise all HTTPS sites will complain about the certificate).

The most practical option for an external browser is the **Firefox + FoxyProxy extension** combo: you set up a proxy profile for `127.0.0.1:8080` and switch it with a single button, without touching system settings. For Chrome it is convenient to spin up a separate profile with a flag:

```bash
# Linux/macOS: a separate Chrome strictly through Burp, a separate profile
google-chrome \
  --proxy-server=http://127.0.0.1:8080 \
  --user-data-dir=/tmp/chrome-burp \
  --ignore-certificate-errors   # handy for a lab, BUT masks real TLS problems
```

> Tip: `--ignore-certificate-errors` is good only for a one-off lab. On a live target it is better to install the Burp CA properly - otherwise you will also swallow the target's real certificate problems and not notice them.

## Typical startup problems (and how to fix them)

- **The browser does not open sites / "unable to connect to the proxy".** Check that Burp is actually listening on the port: **Proxy > Proxy settings > Proxy listeners**, where the line `127.0.0.1:8080` should be lit with status *Running*. If the port is taken by another process - change it to `8081`.
- **HTTPS sites scream about an insecure certificate.** The Burp CA is not installed. For the built-in browser this never happens; for an external one - install the certificate (see the separate entry on the CA).
- **HTTP history is empty even though the site loads.** That means traffic is going around Burp. A frequent cause: the system proxy turned off, a VPN/corporate PAC file intercepted the settings, or the application ignores the system proxy (mobile apps, Electron, Go binaries - see Invisible proxying).
- **It lags/freezes on a large SPA.** Too many **Match and replace** rules are open or response interception is enabled for everything. Turn off the unnecessary, filter by scope.
- **Requests to Google, telemetry, updates get caught.** Set a **Target scope** and enable *"And in-scope items only"* in the filters - this sharply cleans up the noise (see the entry on HTTP history).

> Pitfall from practice: Burp quietly breaks connections that use **client certificate authentication** or strict **certificate pinning** (typical for mobile and desktop applications). Web in a browser is hardly affected by this, but mobile applications often require separate fiddling - pinning has to be removed (Frida/Objection), otherwise traffic simply will not flow into Burp.

## A minimal working cycle

1. **Proxy > Intercept > Open browser**.
2. Walk through the application like a regular user - click, log in, fill out forms.
3. Look at what has accumulated in **Proxy > HTTP history**.
4. An interesting request → right-click → **Send to Repeater** (to play with by hand) or **Send to Intruder** (to brute-force).
5. Turn on **Intercept** pointedly, when you need to catch and change a specific request.

> Important: until you are comfortable - run Proxy only against test/non-production systems. Intercepting and editing traffic easily breaks multi-step scenarios (payments, transfers, imports), and on production this can lead to real consequences.

entries.title: Intercept: Forward / Drop, editing on the fly
entries.body:
# Intercept - intercepting and editing traffic on the fly

The **Proxy > Intercept** tab puts traffic "on pause": a request (and, if you want, the response) freezes in Burp, and until you release it, it will not go anywhere. This gives a window in which you can read the message and rewrite it by hand before it reaches the server or the browser.

## Buttons you need to know

- **Intercept is on / Intercept is off** - the main toggle. When **Intercept is on** is lit, Burp holds messages. When **off** - everything passes straight through and is simply written to **HTTP history**.
- **Forward** - release the current (selected) message onward. The default hotkey is `Ctrl+F`.
- **Drop** - discard the message, it will NEVER reach the server. This is not "undo", it is precisely "destroy the request".
- **Action** (or right-click in the editor) - the actions menu: **Send to Repeater**, **Send to Intruder**, **Do intercept > Response to this request**, etc.

> Through the dropdown arrows next to the buttons there are **Forward all** and **Drop all** - release/discard at once everything that has accumulated in the interception queue. Handy when a dozen background requests are stuck and you only need the next one.

## How to edit a REQUEST

1. Turn on **Intercept is on**.
2. In the browser perform an action (submit a form, click a button).
3. The request will freeze on the **Intercept** tab. Right in the editor change anything - the method, path, headers, body, cookie.
4. Click **Forward** - the modified request will go to the server.

Example: we intercepted an email-change request and replaced `userId` with someone else's (an IDOR/BOLA check):

```http
POST /api/v2/account/email HTTP/1.1
Host: shop.example.com
Cookie: session=eyJhbGciOi...
Content-Type: application/json
Content-Length: 57

{"userId": 1337, "email": "attacker@evil.tld"}
```

We change `1337` to `1338` right in the body → **Forward** → look at the response. If the email/change went through for someone else's account - we found an IDOR.

## How to edit a RESPONSE

By default Burp intercepts only requests. To catch a response:

- At the moment when the request is frozen on **Intercept**, click **Action > Do intercept > Response to this request**. After **Forward** Burp will stop the server's response too, and it can also be rewritten before being handed to the browser.
- Or enable interception of all responses: **Proxy > Proxy settings > Response interception rules > Intercept responses**.

Why this matters in hunting:

- **Remove client-side restrictions.** A field is disabled, a button is hidden, an "admin only" menu item is hidden by JS. We catch the response and edit the flag:

```http
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json

{"user":{"role":"user","isAdmin":false,"canDelete":false}}
```

We change `false` to `true` → the admin buttons unlock in the UI. This in itself is not a vulnerability (the server still decides), but it is a quick way to **reach hidden functionality** and fire off a request that you then check on the server.

- **Bypass client-side step validation** in wizards, open hidden feature flags, slip in a test banner, etc.

## When to turn it on, and when to turn it off

The main rule: **keep Intercept off almost always.** If you leave it on, Burp will hold EVERY request - a modern site will immediately flood you with dozens of background calls, and you will drown pressing Forward.

Working tactics:

- **Intercept off** - while you just walk through the application and accumulate **HTTP history**.
- **Intercept on** - turn it on right before the action you want to catch and change. Caught it → changed it → Forward → immediately turn it back off.
- For repeated experiments on a single request, interception is not needed at all - send it to **Repeater** and hammer there as much as you like, without touching the browser.

> Pitfall: if the application "froze" and nothing loads - first thing, check whether **Intercept is on** is lit with a clogged queue. A very frequent cause of "Burp broke everything for me".

> Tip: to avoid disabling interception while not catching junk, set up rules in **Proxy settings** - for example, do not intercept requests to static assets (`.js`, `.css`, `.png`, fonts) and responses with those types. Then **Intercept on** will catch only meaningful requests.

entries.title: HTTP history: filters, search, sending to Repeater/Intruder
entries.body:
# HTTP history - a log of all traffic

**Proxy > HTTP history** is a table into which Burp writes every "request-response" pair that passed through the proxy, regardless of whether interception was on or not. In practice this is your main working screen: from here you look for interesting entry points, send requests to other tools, and reconstruct the picture of how the application works.

The columns I look at first: `#` (sequence number), `Host`, `Method`, `URL`, `Status code`, `Length` (response size), `MIME type`, `Params` (whether there are parameters), `Cookies`, `Time`. Columns can be toggled on/off and sorted by clicking the header.

## Filters - without them you cannot make sense of the noise

Above the table there is a filter bar (**Filter**). Clicking it expands a panel. What really saves time:

- **Filter by request type** → the **"Show only in-scope items"** checkbox. First set a **Target > Scope** (right-click on the host → *Add to scope*), then enable this checkbox - and the history instantly clears of Google, CDN, telemetry, and other extraneous stuff.
- **Filter by MIME type** - clear the checkboxes for `CSS`, `Image`, `Script`, `Font`, `Other binary`, leave `HTML`, `JSON`, `XML`. This way you cut off static assets and see only meaningful responses.
- **Filter by status code** - for example, show only `2xx`+`3xx`, or conversely fish out all the `500`s (bugs often hide behind them) and `403`s (an access boundary - candidates for a bypass).
- **Filter by search term** - substring/regex over the request and response.
- **Filter by listener / annotations / extensions** - by proxy listener, by notes/color, by what an extension added.

```
# Quick recipe "remove all the noise":
1. Target → right-click the domain → Add to scope
2. HTTP history → Filter → Show only in-scope items ✔
3. Filter → MIME type → clear Script/CSS/Image/Font
```

## Searching the history

The **Search** field (the magnifier icon, or `Ctrl+Shift+F` in the window) searches the bodies of requests and responses across the entire history at once. Very useful queries during recon:

```
authorization        # where tokens/Bearer stick out
password|passwd|pwd   # credential leaks in responses/logs (regex)
api_key|apikey|secret # keys in JS bundles and responses
debug|stacktrace|sql  # traces of debug and SQL errors
s3.amazonaws.com      # buckets, links to the cloud
```

> Tip: search for internal hosts, versions, comments, and endpoints right in the responses. JS bundles that have passed through Proxy are a goldmine of hidden API paths; pulling them out via a history search is faster than by eye.

## Sending to other tools

Right-clicking a row (or several selected ones) gives an actions menu:

- **Send to Repeater** (`Ctrl+R`) - send the request to **Repeater** to fiddle with by hand: change parameters, headers, check IDOR/SQLi/logic.
- **Send to Intruder** (`Ctrl+I`) - to **Intruder** for automated brute-forcing (parameter fuzzing, brute force, id enumeration).
- **Send to Organizer** - set aside in "favorites" so as not to lose a promising request.
- **Copy URL / Copy as curl command** - pull the request outward, for example for a PoC in the report.
- **Show response in browser** - open the response in a browser (handy for reflections and checking the render).

A typical hunting flow: found a request with `id=` in the history → **Send to Repeater**, changed the id by hand, saw someone else's data → confirmed IDOR → **Send to Intruder**, ran through a range of ids, gathered the scale → all of this, with notes and highlights, went into the report.

## Highlights and notes

Right-click → **Highlight** colors the row in one of the colors; **Add note** attaches a text comment (the **Notes** column). I keep a simple color scheme, for example:

- 🔴 red - a confirmed or nearly confirmed vulnerability;
- 🟡 yellow - "need to come back, looks suspicious";
- 🟢 green - checked, clean/not interesting.

Notes and colors sync between **Proxy**, **Repeater**, and **Intruder** - mark something in one place and it is visible everywhere. This greatly helps not to lose the thread on a large target, where the history grows to thousands of rows.

> Pitfall: the history lives in the project's memory. On a temporary project everything will vanish when Burp is closed. For a long engagement, create a **disk-based project** (Pro), otherwise you will lose both the history and the notes.

entries.title: Match and replace: automatic substitution in traffic
entries.body:
# Match and replace - automatic substitution in every request/response

**Match and replace** is a set of rules that Burp applies to PASSING traffic automatically, without interception. Set a rule once - and from then on Burp rewrites every message by the template itself. This removes the routine: you do not need to catch and edit the same header by hand for the hundredth time.

The settings are in **Proxy > Proxy settings > Match and replace** (in recent versions - also **Settings > Tools > Proxy**). Click **Add** to create a rule.

## Rule fields

- **Type** - which part of the message to apply to. The main values:
  - `Request header` - request header
  - `Request body` - request body
  - `Request first line` - the request start line (`GET /path HTTP/1.1`)
  - `Request param name` / `Request param value` - parameter name/value
  - `Response header` - response header
  - `Response body` - response body
  - `Response first line` - the response status line
- **Match** - what we search for (a string or a regex). For headers, you usually write the whole header name here.
- **Replace** - what we replace it with.
- **Regex match** - treat **Match** as a regular expression.
- **Comment** - the rule's label (I strongly advise labeling them - otherwise in an hour you will forget what is enabled).

The logic for headers: if **Match** matched an existing header - it is **replaced** with **Replace**. If you leave **Match** empty (or specify a nonexistent header) and write a ready `Name: value` in **Replace** - the header is **added**. To **delete** a header - write it in **Match** and leave **Replace** empty.

## Real rules I keep on hand

**1. User-Agent substitution** (pretend to be a mobile/bot, check the mobile version or other logic):

```
Type:    Request header
Match:   ^User-Agent.*$
Replace: User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (iPhone; CPU iPhone OS 17_0 like Mac OS X) AppleWebKit/605.1.15
Regex:   ✔
```

**2. X-Forwarded-For spoofing** (an attempt to pose as an internal/trusted IP, bypass IP restrictions, reach an admin panel via "localhost"):

```
Type:    Request header
Match:   (leave empty)
Replace: X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1
Regex:   ✘
```

Often in a single rule it makes sense to pile up a whole set of "client IP" headers - applications and proxies read them differently:

```
Type:    Request header
Replace: X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1
```
```
Type:    Request header
Replace: X-Real-IP: 127.0.0.1
```
```
Type:    Request header
Replace: X-Originating-IP: 127.0.0.1
```

> Scenario: the `/admin` endpoint returns `403` from outside, but is "accessible only from the local network". You add a set of `X-Forwarded-For/X-Real-IP/X-Originating-IP: 127.0.0.1`, browse the site as usual - and if somewhere the filtering trusts these headers, you get access without any interception.

**3. A persistent bypass header / tracking your own requests** (for example, a marker for a WAF bypass or a bug hunter identifier in Bug Bounty per the program's rules):

```
Type:    Request header
Replace: X-Bug-Hunter: hackerone-username
```

**4. Removing client-side restrictions via the response** - replace a flag right in the JSON response so the front-end unlocks functionality:

```
Type:    Response body
Match:   "isAdmin":false
Replace: "isAdmin":true
Regex:   ✘
```

or remove the `disabled` attribute from all form fields:

```
Type:    Response body
Match:   \s+disabled(=["']?[^"'>\s]*["']?)?
Replace: (empty)
Regex:   ✔
```

**5. Removing frame protection to check for Clickjacking** (strip out the headers that prevent embedding the page in an iframe locally):

```
Type:    Response header
Match:   ^X-Frame-Options.*$
Replace: (empty)
Regex:   ✔
```
```
Type:    Response header
Match:   (?i)content-security-policy.*frame-ancestors[^;]*;?
Replace: (empty)
Regex:   ✔
```

**6. Forcibly disable response caching** (to see fresh data, not from cache):

```
Type:    Response header
Replace: Cache-Control: no-store, no-cache, must-revalidate
```

## Tips and pitfalls

- **Label every rule** in **Comment** and keep enabled exactly what you need right now. The most frequent pain is a forgotten active rule because of which "the target behaves strangely", and you spend an hour looking for a bug that does not exist.
- **Match and replace is global** - it hits ALL hosts passing through the proxy, not just the target. If a rule is aggressive (for example, it breaks headers), it will affect extraneous sites too. Where possible, make **Match** narrow.
- **Substitution in a response ≠ a vulnerability.** You change only what your browser sees. This is a tool for recon and access to hidden functionality; always do the final check against the server (via **Repeater**).
- **Regex is greedy by default** - watch out for `.*`, so as not to accidentally grab half the message. Test a rule on a single request in **Repeater** before sending it into battle.
- The rules apply to traffic from both the built-in browser and an external one - the main thing is that it goes through this Burp listener.

entries.title: HTTPS and the Burp CA certificate: installation and TLS errors
entries.body:
# HTTPS and the Burp CA certificate

For Burp to be able to read and edit HTTPS traffic, it has to break TLS: on the fly it generates a fake certificate for each site, signed by its own root authority (**Burp CA**). The browser will see a certificate issued by "Burp CA" and, if that CA is not marked as trusted, will raise an alarm. So the whole point of setting up HTTPS comes down to one thing: **make the Burp CA trusted in the client**.

> With the built-in browser (**Open browser**) you do not need to do anything at all - there the Burp CA is already trusted. Everything below is about **external** browsers, mobile devices, and third-party clients.

## Step 1. Download the Burp CA certificate

1. Point the browser at the proxy (`127.0.0.1:8080`).
2. In this browser open the address: `http://burp` (precisely http, precisely through the proxy).
3. At the top right click **CA Certificate** - the file `cacert.der` will download.

This is Burp's public root certificate (without the client's private key) - it is the one you need to install as a trusted root.

## Step 2. Install into the browser/OS

**Firefox** (its own store, ignores the OS):
```
Settings → Privacy & Security → Certificates → View Certificates
→ Authorities → Import… → select cacert.der
→ check "Trust this CA to identify websites" → OK
```

**Chrome/Edge (Windows)** - they use the system store:
```
double-click cacert.der → Install Certificate
→ Local Machine → Place all certificates in the following store
→ Trusted Root Certification Authorities → Finish
```
(or import via `certmgr.msc` → *Trusted Root Certification Authorities*)

**Linux (system-wide):** Burp conveniently hands out PEM. Convert DER→PEM and place it into the system:
```bash
openssl x509 -inform DER -in cacert.der -out burp.pem
sudo cp burp.pem /usr/local/share/ca-certificates/burp.crt
sudo update-ca-certificates
```

**macOS:** double-click `cacert.der` → add to **Keychain (System)** → find "PortSwigger CA" → *Get Info* → **Trust → Always Trust**.

## Step 3. Verification

Go to any `https://` site through Burp. If the lock is green/neutral and there are no warnings - the CA is installed correctly, and all HTTPS is now readable in **HTTP history**.

## Mobile devices

The general scheme: the phone and the computer are on the same network, and a listener on "all interfaces" is raised in Burp.

1. **Burp:** Proxy settings → Proxy listeners → Add → Bind to port `8080`, **Bind to address: All interfaces**.
2. **Phone, Wi-Fi:** proxy manually → the computer's IP, port `8080`.
3. **Certificate:** on the phone open `http://burp`, download the certificate and install it.

- **Android.** Modern Android trusts user CAs only if the application explicitly allowed it (`networkSecurityConfig`). For system applications and most third-party ones you will have to place the certificate into the **system** store (root needed) or patch the application. Android also requires the certificate in PEM format with the correct hash-based name:
```bash
# turn a DER into a "system" CA for Android (root needed)
openssl x509 -inform DER -in cacert.der -out cacert.pem
hash=$(openssl x509 -inform PEM -subject_hash_old -in cacert.pem | head -1)
mv cacert.pem ${hash}.0
# copy into /system/etc/security/cacerts/ on a rooted device
```
- **iOS.** Install the certificate profile, then it is MANDATORY to enable full trust: *Settings → General → About → Certificate Trust Settings* → flip the toggle for the PortSwigger CA. Without this second step HTTPS still will not work.

> Pitfall of mobile testing: even with the correct certificate, many applications use **certificate pinning** and flatly refuse to trust the Burp CA. It is cured by removing pinning via **Frida/Objection** (`objection ... android sslpinning disable`) or by patching the APK. If in Burp "nothing flows over HTTPS even though the certificate is installed" - it is almost certainly pinning.

## Common TLS errors and what they mean

- `Your connection is not private` / `SEC_ERROR_UNKNOWN_ISSUER` - the Burp CA is not installed or installed into the wrong store. The most frequent cause.
- Installed it in Firefox, but Chrome still complains - Firefox uses its own store, Chrome the system one; install in both or work in one.
- **`unrecognized_name` / SNI errors, handshake drops** - the target is sensitive to SNI; sometimes TLS settings in Burp or disabling TLS normalization help. In **Repeater** there is **Override SNI**.
- **The site works in a normal browser but breaks through Burp** - **client certificate auth** is possible (you need to load the client certificate into *Settings → TLS → Client TLS certificates*) or pinning.
- After reinstalling/updating Burp HTTPS suddenly broke - Burp's **CA changed**. Regenerate and reinstall the certificate (*Settings → TLS → Proxy listeners*, CA regeneration).

> Security: the Burp CA is, in essence, a key with which you can sign "any" site for your machine. Do not carry the same CA private key between machines and do not leave it trusted forever on devices you use in everyday life. After the test it is best to delete the certificate.

entries.title: Invisible proxying: non-proxy-aware clients
entries.body:
# Invisible proxying - for clients that cannot do proxies

Burp's normal mode is designed for a **proxy-aware** client: the browser knows there is a proxy and sends it special requests (in HTTP - with the full URL in the first line, for HTTPS - a preliminary `CONNECT`). But many clients cannot do this: thick desktop applications, mobile applications, scripts, IoT gadgets, custom binaries. They simply open a connection "straight to the server" and send an ordinary relative request:

```http
GET /api/login HTTP/1.1
Host: api.example.com
```

If such a request arrives at a normal Burp listener, it gets confused: there is no full URL in the line, there was no `CONNECT` - Burp does not know which real host to forward this to. This is exactly what **Invisible proxying** (invisible/transparent proxying) exists for: Burp starts trusting the `Host` header (and SNI for TLS) and figures out where to forward by itself.

## When you need it

- The application **ignores the system proxy** (typical for Java/Electron/Go, many mobile apps and desktop clients).
- You have **forcibly redirected traffic to Burp** - via editing `hosts`, DNS, iptables/pf, or a transparent gateway - and the client thinks it is talking directly to the server.
- You need to intercept the traffic of a client that has no proxy settings at all.

## How to set it up (the scheme)

The idea: force the client's traffic to arrive at Burp, and have Burp work in invisible mode.

1. **Redirect the client to Burp.** The simplest way is to substitute the target's address with `127.0.0.1` (or the IP of the machine with Burp) in the client's hosts file:
```
# /etc/hosts (or C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts)
127.0.0.1   api.example.com
```
Now the client will hammer the local port, thinking it is `api.example.com`.

2. **Raise a listener in invisible mode.** Since the client goes to standard ports, the Burp listener is usually hung on `80` and `443`:
```
Proxy → Proxy settings → Proxy listeners → Add
  Bind to port:    443        (and a separate listener on 80)
  Bind to address: All interfaces  (or Loopback only - depends on the setup)
  Request handling → Support invisible proxying ✔
```

3. **(For HTTPS) sort out the target host.** In invisible mode Burp determines the target by `Host`/SNI. If that is not enough or you want to firmly fix the upstream - on the same **Request handling** tab enable **Redirect to host/port** and write in the server's real address:
```
Request handling:
  Redirect to host: api.example.com
  Redirect to port: 443
  Force use of TLS ✔   (if the upstream is over HTTPS)
```

4. **Certificate.** HTTPS still requires the Burp CA to be trusted by the client (see the entry on the CA). For desktop - install the CA into the system; for mobile - into the device's store (and remember about pinning).

## Practical scenario

You are testing a thick client that talks to `api.vendor.local:443` and does not react to the system proxy:

1. In the client's `hosts`: `127.0.0.1  api.vendor.local`.
2. In Burp: a listener on `443`, **All interfaces**, **Support invisible proxying ✔**, **Redirect to host: the_server's_real_IP**, **Force use of TLS ✔**.
3. You install the Burp CA into the client's system.
4. You launch the application → its API traffic appears in **HTTP history**, then as usual - **Repeater/Intruder**.

> On Linux, instead of editing hosts it is convenient to funnel traffic through iptables to Burp, and on mobile - to raise Burp as a Wi-Fi proxy/gateway. The essence is the same: the client believes it is talking directly, while Burp stands invisibly in the middle.

## Pitfalls

- **Loops / "onto itself".** If in hosts you funneled the host to `127.0.0.1`, and Burp tries to forward by that same `Host` to the same place - you get a loop. That is why in invisible mode, when substituting hosts, you usually need **Redirect to host** with the REAL address (or IP) of the upstream, so Burp knows where to actually send.
- **Several hosts on one port.** Without SNI/`Host` Burp will not distinguish virtual hosts - invisible mode relies precisely on these fields; with old clients that lack SNI there can be problems.
- **Pinning and client-cert** have not gone anywhere - invisible solves only the "client does not know about the proxy" problem, but not the problem of trusting the certificate.
- **Do not leave an invisible listener on 80/443 enabled in the background** - it may unexpectedly intercept local traffic and interfere with other applications.
